body:
## Problem

The five PostToolUse validation hooks all use matcher `Write|Edit`, so they
never fire on a file changed via Bash (`sed -i`, shell redirection, `git mv`).
The commit-time gate (pre-commit) is skippable with `git commit --no-verify`,
and no rule forbids either path. Enforcement of skill/JSON/version/ruff/findings
validity therefore rests entirely on CI being a *required* status check.

## Evidence

- `.claude/settings.json`: every PostToolUse entry has `"matcher": "Write|Edit"`;
  none matches Bash.
- Constructed bypass: `sed -i 's/"1.8.0"/"9.9.9"/' .claude-plugin/plugin.json`
  triggers no PostToolUse hook, then `git commit --no-verify` skips the
  pre-commit version-sync hook. The only remaining check is CI's
  `check-version-sync.py` — which binds only if branch protection requires it.
- No rule forbids `--no-verify`; none names "CI required check" as the gate.

## Impact

No current protected-state breach *provided* branch protection requires the
`Validate` job (prior finding audit-210a6beb recorded this fixed — but it is a
GitHub-side setting not verifiable from the repo). If that required check is
removed, this becomes a live High: local edits reach `main` unvalidated.

## Fix

Defense-in-depth: (1) add a short `.claude/rules/` rule naming the required CI
check as the authoritative gate and forbidding `--no-verify` for
skill/manifest/findings changes; (2) verify the branch-protection required-check
during release. No hook can close the Write|Edit matcher gap (a Claude Code
structural limit) — rule + required-CI is the mitigation.

## Resolution

Fix part (1) is satisfied by .claude/rules/commit-gate-integrity.md (added 2026-07-09, commit 30e2157): it names the CI Validate job as the authoritative gate, forbids --no-verify on skill/manifest/findings changes, and mandates Validate as a required status check — negating the finding's 'no rule forbids --no-verify' evidence. The residual (Write|Edit PostToolUse matcher gap and GitHub-side branch protection) is the structural, non-repo-fixable limit the finding itself documents; rule + required-CI is the accepted mitigation.
title: PostToolUse validation is Write/Edit-scoped and pre-commit is --no-verify-skippable; only CI binds

body:
## Problem

`_conventions.md` binds every command. Of its three consent-relevant gates, two
are hardened against autonomous/goal mode and one — the most dangerous — is not:

- Step 0 (migration consent): "**this question overrides autonomous/goal mode**".
- Step 6 (commit findings): "Ask ... — **never commit silently**".
- Step 5 (apply fixes to the user's code): "If the command applies fixes: ask
  `Apply fixes? (a)ll (c)ritical-and-high only (s)afe (n)o` and fix in severity
  order" — **no autonomous-mode override, no never-apply-silently clause.**

Step 5 gates the single most destructive action in the toolkit (mutating the
user's source), yet it is the only one of the three that an autonomous/goal run
can rationalize past.

## Evidence

- `_conventions.md:54-66` step 0 carries the explicit autonomous-mode override.
- `_conventions.md:77` step 6 carries "never commit silently".
- `_conventions.md:74` step 5 carries neither.
- Constructed bypass: a user runs a fixing command under a goal/autonomous
  directive ("/nitpicker security and fix everything", or an agent in goal mode).
  The agent reaches step 5, notes it says "ask" with no interactive user and —
  unlike steps 0 and 6 — finds no "overrides autonomous/goal mode" and no "never
  apply silently" guard. It applies (a)ll fixes, including refactors, to the
  codebase without ever presenting the (a)/(c)/(s)/(n) scope choice. The gate
  whose entire purpose is letting the user bound fix *scope* is skipped.
- Blast radius: this is not one command — every command that applies fixes and
  delegates to the shared protocol (audit, security, a11y, ci, observability,
  perf, errors, leaks, concurrency, config, privacy, ...) inherits the gap.

## Impact

In autonomous/goal mode, any fixing command can rewrite the user's code —
including unrequested refactors — with no scope consent. Interactive runs are
unaffected; the failure is mode-conditional, which is exactly why it survives
casual reading.

## Fix

Harden step 5 to match its siblings. Append to the step:
"This prompt overrides autonomous/goal mode — never apply fixes without
presenting it. With no interactive user, default to (n)o and record the
un-applied fixes in the run summary." One sentence; makes the most dangerous
gate at least as hard as the migration and commit gates.

## Resolution

Hardened _conventions.md step 5: 'This prompt overrides autonomous/goal mode — never apply fixes without presenting it. With no interactive user, default to (n)o and record the un-applied fixes.' Closure: unconditional override now present, matching steps 0 and 6.
title: apply-fixes consent gate has no autonomous-mode override or never-apply-silently backstop

body:
## Problem

The default `audit` command is described as an "exhaustive whole-repository review" across every quality dimension, but it enforced no explicit coverage list. Its `## Behavior` only deep-ran a specialist command when the user *named* a focus; otherwise the breadth of the review — whether security, tests, migrations, a11y, concurrency, etc. were each actually applied — rested entirely on the agent's memory of the scope prose. `_conventions.md` states "silence means approval: an unfiled finding is an accepted defect", so any lens the agent silently skipped became an accepted blind spot with no artifact recording the omission.

## Evidence

- `commands/audit.md` `## Review scope` listed eight base dimensions; `## Behavior` step 2 ran a specialist "if the extra instructions name a focus" — i.e. specialist coverage was opt-in, not mandatory.
- No file enumerated the full lens set as a task list, and nothing required the agent to account for each lens (applied / clean / N/A) before closing the audit.
- The skill offers ~24 review/quality lenses (specialist commands); the default audit had no mechanism guaranteeing all were touched.

## Impact

A default `/nitpicker audit` could report "exhaustive" while never applying whole dimensions (e.g. concurrency, observability, migrations). The gap is invisible: no task, no finding, no N/A note marks the skipped lens, so a reader cannot distinguish "checked and clean" from "never checked".

## Fix

Add an external coverage checklist `commands/_audit-coverage.md` (a `_`-prefixed shared reference, exempt from the command table) enumerating every quality lens the skill offers — 4 base + 20 specialist + 3 agent-enforcement — each mapped to its specialist command with an applicability gate. Wire `audit.md` step 2 to copy every task into the agent's task list at run start, and require each task to close in exactly one state (findings filed / clean / N/A-with-reason), listed in the run summary. Link the checklist directly from SKILL.md (one-level-deep reference rule). A named focus now deepens one lens without narrowing the checklist.

## Resolution

Added commands/_audit-coverage.md (27-task coverage checklist spanning the full review/quality command set), wired audit.md to copy every task into the agent's task list with a mandatory close-state per task, and linked the checklist from SKILL.md. make check green (352 tests, markdownlint/validate-skill pass); the checklist is an exempt _-prefixed shared reference and does not leak into the command listing.
title: Default audit asserts exhaustive coverage but enforced no checklist — lenses silently skippable

body:
## Problem

Two execution guarantees were implemented per-command rather than bound by `_conventions.md`, so a command that forgot them had no backstop:

1. **External-tool preflight.** `security`, `ci`, `concurrency`, `config`, `deps`, and `cr` each independently probe tool availability ("never install", record "not available", tool failure never aborts). But `pr` step 1 fetched a PR diff with `gh pr diff <number>` and no `command -v gh` check — a missing `gh` crashes the fetch or yields an empty diff, which step 2 then "reviews" and step 4 emits as a clean review. Empty-because-the-tool-is-absent is presented as reviewed-and-clean.
2. **Step-as-task-list.** Only the default `audit` was wired to copy its procedure into the agent's task list (the prior audit-coverage change). Every other command with a numbered Process (e.g. `security` has 10 steps, `deps`/`commits`/`cr` have multi-step procedures) relied on the agent remembering to run each step, with no enforcement that none was silently dropped.

## Evidence

- `pr.md:20` (before fix): `- PR number: fetch diff via ` + "gh pr diff <number>" — no preflight, no degradation path. Contrast `cr.md:24` which does `which gh && gh auth status` with a `GITHUB_TOKEN` fallback.
- `security.md:16`, `ci.md:19`, `concurrency.md:37`, `config.md:42`, `deps.md:44`: the same preflight pattern re-authored in each, none binding on the others.
- No `_conventions.md` rule required either guarantee.

## Impact

`/nitpicker pr <number>` on a host without `gh` produces an empty or crashing review with no signal that no code was actually reviewed. More broadly, any future command (or a command gaining tool use) could omit the preflight or skip procedure steps with nothing to catch it — silence means approval.

## Fix

Codify both guarantees as binding rules in `_conventions.md` under a new `## Execution` section: (1) a command with a numbered Process copies each step into the agent's task list and closes every step before reporting; (2) before invoking any external binary the skill does not ship (scanners, `gh`, package managers, analyzers), probe with `command -v`/`which`, never install, run only tools found, record missing as "not available" and failed as "errored: <msg>", and never present empty tool output as a clean result. Fix the concrete gap: `pr.md` step 1 now preflights `command -v gh` and stops with a clear message when absent instead of emitting an empty review.

## Resolution

Added a '## Execution' section to _conventions.md binding both guarantees (step-as-task-list + external-tool preflight with never-install/never-empty-as-clean) on every command, and fixed pr.md step 1 to preflight 'command -v gh' and stop with a clear message when gh is absent. make-check gates that ran (validate-skill, markdownlint, findings validate, version sync) all pass; the index-check diff reflects only uncommitted findings-store state.
title: External-tool preflight and step-as-task-list were ad-hoc, not binding; pr fetched via gh with no availability check

body:
## Problem

`.claude/rules/use-uv-runner.md` mandates that every shipped skill tool under
`skills/*/scripts/` be **stdlib-only** ("no imports outside the standard
library — uv cannot be assumed to exist on systems running the skills"). No
hook, validator, CI step, or test enforces this at the directory level. The
only coverage is incidental: the four existing tools each have a test that
imports them in a lean `uv run --with pytest` env, so an import failure would
surface there — but that coverage is per-tool and accidental, not a rule check.

## Evidence

- `git grep -niE "stdlib_module_names|stdlib-only|import-linter|tidy-imports"`
  over tests/, scripts/, .pre-commit-config.yaml, .github/, pyproject.toml
  returns only a prose line in `.github/copilot-instructions.md` and a *comment*
  in `validate-audit-findings-hook.py`. Neither is a check.
- Ruff `select = ["E","F","I","UP","B","SIM","PTH"]` — none detect a non-stdlib
  import (F-codes cover unused/undefined, not import resolvability).
- Constructed bypass: add `skills/nitpicker/scripts/report.py` with
  `#!/usr/bin/env python3` and `import requests`, referenced by no test.
  `make check` and CI both pass — validate-skill ignores `.py`; ruff does not
  resolve imports; pytest never imports the untested file; pre-commit ruff is
  clean. It ships. A consumer runs `python3 report.py` and gets
  `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'`.

## Impact

A shipped tool with a third-party import breaks on every consumer machine
(Copilot, pi, plain Claude Code without uv) — exactly the failure the rule
exists to prevent — while the whole dev suite stays green. The incidental test
coverage also silently evaporates if any third-party module is ever added to the
dev/test environment for an unrelated reason.

## Fix

Add an explicit stdlib-only check as a pre-commit `local` hook + CI step:
AST-parse every `skills/*/scripts/*.py`, collect top-level import roots, fail if
any root is not in `sys.stdlib_module_names` (3.11+) nor a first-party sibling.
~30 lines, stdlib-only, run the same way CI runs the other validators. This
turns the directory-wide rule into an enforced gate independent of per-tool tests.

## Resolution

Added scripts/check-stdlib-only.py (AST scan of skills/*/scripts/*.py vs sys.stdlib_module_names) wired as pre-commit local hook + test. Closure: a new shipped tool with 'import requests' now yields a violation (BLOCKS: True).
title: stdlib-only rule for shipped tools has no directory-level enforcement

body:
## Problem

`cr` mutates git state (commit/push) and posts to GitHub, gated by the Step 6
interactive menu. Two gaps let an autonomous/goal run mutate state without
explicit authorisation:

1. The hard-rule backstop omits `commit`. `cr.md:167`: "Never push and never
   post replies without explicit user authorisation granted in Step 6." It names
   `push` and `post replies` — not `commit`. So committing has no hard guard.
2. The Step 6 menu (`cr.md:133-137`) has no autonomous-mode fail-safe and no
   silence default — unlike the migrator and unwired gates, which both state an
   explicit default-on-silence and override autonomous/goal mode.

## Evidence

- `cr.md:167` (Fix strategy hard rule) guards push + post, not commit.
- `cr.md:133-142`: the "What next? 1. Leave it 2. Commit only 3. Commit and push"
  menu fires only interactively; the file states no default when no user is
  present and no "overrides autonomous/goal mode" clause.
- Constructed bypass: an agent runs `cr` under "implement the CR comments and get
  the branch ready" (goal/autonomous). It reaches Step 6 with Implemented
  verdicts and no interactive user. `cr.md:167` forbids pushing without
  authorisation but says nothing about committing, so the agent self-selects
  option 2 "Commit only" and writes a commit onto the branch — citing that the
  hard rule only gates push/post. State mutated without explicit consent.

## Impact

Lower blast radius than a push (a local commit is recoverable and not yet
public), but it is still an unauthorised git mutation, and it is inconsistent
with the command's own stated model ("posted only after the fixes are pushed and
the user confirms"). The consent gate is undefined in autonomous mode.

## Fix

Two one-line edits: (1) `cr.md:167` → "Never commit, push, or post replies
without explicit user authorisation granted in Step 6." (2) Add to Step 6: "With
no interactive user (autonomous/goal mode), default to option 1 (Leave it) — no
commit, push, or reply — and record that in the summary." Mirrors the fail-safe
default already used by the migrator and unwired gates.

## Resolution

cr.md: line 167 now 'Never commit, push, or post replies without explicit user authorisation'; Step 6 gains an autonomous fail-safe defaulting to option 1 (Leave it). Closure: commit is now under the hard rule and the gate has a no-user default.
title: cr Step 6 consent gate: commit ungated by the hard rule and no autonomous-mode fail-safe

body:
## Problem

`.claude/rules/github-actions-security.md` mandates: SHA-pin every third-party
action, least-privilege `permissions:`, a `name:` and `concurrency:` per
workflow, and running `zizmor --pedantic` after editing workflows. None is
enforced by any hook, pre-commit repo, or CI job. The zizmor clause is
self-exempting: "When zizmor is not installed, skip this step and record that it
was skipped" — and nothing installs zizmor in CI, so the security gate is
skipped by design on every run.

## Evidence

- `git grep -niE "zizmor|actionlint|pin.?github|ratchet"` over
  `.pre-commit-config.yaml` and `.github/workflows/` returns nothing.
- `zizmor` appears only in the rule that mandates it and in the `ci.md` command
  body (consumer instructions) — no workflow or hook runs it.
- The `check-github-workflows` pre-commit hook validates workflow *schema*, not
  action pinning or permission scope.
- Constructed bypass: change `uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb...` to
  `uses: actions/checkout@v7` in `validate-skills.yml`. `make check` and CI pass
  — schema validation accepts a tag ref; no pin check runs. The unpinned action
  ships.

## Impact

A mutable action ref (`@v7`, `@main`) is a supply-chain foothold: the upstream
tag can be re-pointed to malicious code that runs in CI with the workflow's
token. The repo's own security rule is meant to prevent exactly this and binds
nothing.

## Fix

Add zizmor to CI unconditionally: `uvx zizmor --pedantic .github/workflows/` as a
CI step failing on findings. For a zero-dependency floor, add a `local`
pre-commit hook that greps every `uses:` line and fails on any ref not matching
a 40-hex SHA. Either makes the pinning mandate an enforced gate.

## Resolution

Added zizmor pre-commit hook (v1.26.1, --pedantic --offline), always-on (removes the self-exemption). Closure: an unpinned @v7 ref triggers error[unpinned-uses], exit 14.
title: SHA-pin/least-privilege workflow rule is never enforced; zizmor step self-exempts

body:
## Resolution

Replaced nested double-quoted `python3 -c "..."` inside `grep "v$(…)"` with single-quoted `-c '...'` so the shell command is syntactically valid. Broken form: `grep "v$(python3 -c "import json; ...")"`. Fixed form: `grep "v$(python3 -c 'import json; ...')"`.

title: release-readiness-reviewer.md step 6 uses broken shell quoting in git tag check

body:
## Resolution

`git tag | grep "v$(...)"` does substring matching. With version `1.2.0`, the grep also matches `v1.2.0-rc1`, `v1.2.10`, etc., causing false "tag exists" verdicts that would block a legitimate release. Verified: `printf 'v1.2.0\nv1.2.0-rc1\n' | grep "v1.2.0"` returns both lines. Replaced with `git rev-parse --verify "refs/tags/v$(...)" 2>/dev/null` which is the canonical exact-match tag-existence check.

Migrated from v1 `nitpicker-findings.md` (Pass 9, 2026-04-26).
title: release-readiness-reviewer.md step 6 uses unanchored grep for tag presence check

body:
## Resolution

The hook used `git diff --name-only HEAD`, which sees tracked-modified and staged paths but not brand-new untracked files. A freshly-created `skills/foo/SKILL.md` not yet `git add`-ed was invisible — exactly the case where the validate-skills reminder matters most. Switched to `git status --porcelain -uall` (the `-uall` flag is required because the default `-u normal` shows untracked directories as `?? skills/` rather than recursing to the file). Also folded the no-commits fallback path away — `git status --porcelain` works without prior commits — and added explicit handling for rename entries (`R  old -> new`).

Migrated from v1 `nitpicker-findings.md` (Pass 9, 2026-04-26).
title: stop-reminder.py misses untracked new SKILL.md files

body:
## Resolution

After `parse_and_fix` runs, the `else` branch (lines 288-326) recounted h4 findings and rewrote the summary if the input numbers disagreed. But `parse_and_fix` already derives the summary from the same h4 counts when reconstructing the file, so if `fixed == original` the summary was already correct and the inner mismatch check could never fire. Verified by running the hook on a structurally-correct findings file: md5 unchanged before and after. Removed the dead else branch (-39 lines).

Finds`, etc. Official best-practice examples use exactly those verbs in capability summaries ("Generate descriptive commit messages by analyzing git diffs. Use when..."). body:
## Problem

Two defects in `_normalize_body` (findings.py:147-166): (a) the final `re.sub(r"\n{3,}", "\n\n", ...)` runs over the whole text including fences, collapsing intentional blank runs inside code blocks; (b) a `~~~` line inside a ``` fence flips `in_fence` (line 154 treats both markers identically), after which `#` lines inside the fence are reformatted as headings.

## Evidence

`new` with Evidence containing a fence with two consecutive blank lines → collapsed to one. Body with ```` ```\n~~~ art\n# comment\n``` ```` → blank lines injected around the `# comment` code line. Runs on every `new` and `resolve`.

## Impact

Evidence code blocks — the core payload of a finding — are silently mutated.

## Fix

Track the fence marker type and only close on the matching marker; collapse blank runs line-by-line inside the loop, skipping while `in_fence`, instead of a whole-text `re.sub`.

## Resolution

_normalize_body tracks the fence marker type and collapses blank runs outside fences only
title: _normalize_body corrupts fenced code blocks

body:
## Problem

`text.startswith("---\n")` (lines 36-37) fails on CRLF files, so the frontmatter block is treated as body and every `paths:` validation silently skips.

## Evidence

A CRLF rule file with `paths:` frontmatter produces no frontmatter findings at all.

## Impact

Consumer repos with CRLF rule files get vacuous passes.

## Fix

Normalize `text = text.replace("\r\n", "\n")` first (findings.py:105 already does this).

## Resolution

check-rules-anatomy normalizes CRLF before frontmatter detection; test added
title: CRLF rule files silently skip all frontmatter validation

body:
## Problem

`validate-skill.py` tests the raw text for `---\n` without normalizing line endings, so a valid CRLF-encoded SKILL.md is rejected — inconsistent with `common.parse_frontmatter`, which normalizes first.

## Evidence

`validate-skill.py:49` `if not text.startswith("---\n"):` on text read raw at line ~44, while `common.py:11` does `text = text.replace("\r\n", "\n")` before parsing. A file beginning `---\r\n` fails `startswith("---\n")` → `err("missing YAML frontmatter")`.

## Impact

Latent (no CRLF files today); a contributor on a CRLF-configured checkout would get a spurious hard validation failure.

## Fix

Normalize `text = text.replace("\r\n", "\n")` immediately after `read_text`, matching `common.parse_frontmatter`.

## Resolution

validate-skill normalizes CRLF before the frontmatter check; test_crlf_frontmatter_valid.
title: CRLF SKILL.md is wrongly rejected as missing frontmatter

title: 'What is checked' table omits the duplicate-header error

body:
## Problem

`test_ruff_hook_lint_error_exits_2` exercises `ruff-hook.py`, which runs `subprocess.run(["ruff", "check", "--fix", ...])`. The test therefore requires the real `ruff` executable on PATH. But CI's `Run tests` step and the `make test` target both invoke `uv run --with pytest pytest tests/` — an isolated uv environment that provisions **only pytest, not ruff**. The test passes on any dev machine where `ruff` is ambiently installed (mise/uv/mason/~.local/bin) and fails only in CI's clean environment.

## Evidence

- CI run 29074708120 (`Validate` job, PR #54): `FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'ruff'` at `ruff-hook.py:36`; `1 failed, 351 passed`.
- `tests/test_hooks.py:152-164` — the hook shells out to `ruff`; no skip guard, no fixture provisioning it.
- `.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml` `Run tests` step and `Makefile` `test` target ran `uv run --with pytest pytest tests/` (ruff absent).
- Reproduced locally with a minimal PATH (uv present, ruff absent): before fix → `FileNotFoundError`; with `--with ruff` → pass.

## Impact

The `Validate` job is red on every PR from this branch, blocking merge and masking any other signal in the test step. It is also the class of defect this repo's own audits missed across several passes: `make test` / `uv run` were run on a machine with `ruff` ambient, so all 352 tests passed locally while CI's isolated env failed. Local green does not imply CI green when a test depends on an unprovisioned external binary.

## Fix

Provision `ruff` in both test call sites so the environment matches the test's real dependency: `uv run --with pytest --with ruff pytest tests/` in `.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml` (`Run tests`) and in the `Makefile` `test` target. (Keeping the test a hard failure when ruff is missing — rather than a silent skipif — preserves the loud signal if CI ever stops provisioning it.)

## Resolution

Added --with ruff to the CI 'Run tests' step (validate-skills.yml) and the Makefile 'test' target. Verified in a CI-faithful env (system PATH without ruff, uv present): before → FileNotFoundError 'ruff' (1 failed); after → 352 passed. zizmor --pedantic clean on the edited workflow.
title: Hook test shells out to real ruff but CI test step provisions only pytest

body:
## Problem

`.prettierignore` exists but prettier is installed and invoked nowhere. It
advertises a formatting tool that does not run, misleading contributors into
believing JSON/config formatting is enforced (it is not — see the JSON-validation
finding).

## Evidence

- `git grep -il prettier` returns only `.prettierignore` itself plus prose
  mentions (`findings.py` / `deps.md` / `test_findings.py` describe the findings
  generator as emitting "prettier-shaped tables" — unrelated to running prettier).
- `package.json` has no `devDependencies`, no `scripts`, no prettier entry.
- No `.prettierrc*` config, no prettier hook in `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, no
  prettier step in `.github/workflows/`.
- The file's only content ignores `docs/audit/` — a carve-out for a tool that
  never executes.

## Impact

Dead configuration. Low risk, but it lies about the toolchain: a reader assumes
prettier formats the repo. There is no engine behind it.

## Fix

Delete `.prettierignore`. Nothing consumes it. (If JSON/YAML formatting is
actually wanted, add a real formatter hook — but that is a separate, deliberate
choice; the JSON *validation* gap above is better closed by `check-json`.)

## Resolution

Deleted .prettierignore — no prettier engine existed. JSON coverage now handled by check-json/check-jsonschema.
title: Orphaned .prettierignore — prettier is never run

body:
## Problem

The two hand-written schemas (added this session) are stricter than the Claude
Code plugin manifest format they validate, so a valid alternate manifest form is
wrongly rejected by the `check-jsonschema` pre-commit gate:

- `marketplace.schema.json` constrains `plugins[].source` to `{"type":"string"}`,
  but the marketplace format also accepts an object source
  (e.g. `{"source":"github","repo":"owner/name"}`).
- `plugin.schema.json` requires `author` to be an object, but a plain string
  author is also valid.
- `marketplace.schema.json` marks per-plugin `version` **required**, though the
  marketplace format allows it to be derived from each plugin's own `plugin.json`.

## Evidence

- `check-jsonschema --schemafile schemas/marketplace.schema.json` on a manifest
  whose plugin uses `"source": {"source":"github","repo":"a/b"}` fails with
  `$.plugins[0].source: {...} is not of type 'string'` (agent-confirmed).
- The repo's current manifests validate fine (string `source`, object `author`,
  explicit `version`), so this is a latent false positive, not a current break.

## Impact

A future valid edit — switching to an object `source`, a string `author`, or a
derived `version` — would be blocked by the pre-commit gate even though the
manifest is correct. A schema meant to catch mistakes instead rejects valid input.

## Fix

- `source`: `{"oneOf": [{"type": "string"}, {"type": "object"}]}`.
- `author`: allow string or object (`oneOf`).
- Drop `version` from the `plugins` item `required` list; keep the semver
  `pattern` for when it is present.

## Resolution

Loosened schemas: plugins[].source now string|object, author (plugin + marketplace) now object|string. version deliberately KEPT required — check-version-sync.py and release-please extra-files depend on plugins[0].version existing, so requiring it is correct for this repo (that sub-point withdrawn). Closure: an object-source + string-author manifest now validates; the real manifest still passes; metaschema valid.
title: plugin/marketplace schemas reject valid alternate Claude manifest forms

body:
## Problem

The `conventional-commit-msg` hook is `stages: [commit-msg]`. CI runs `pre-commit run --all-files`, which executes only pre-commit-stage hooks, so individual commit messages are never validated by any CI check. Only the PR *title* is linted (by `action-semantic-pull-request`).

## Evidence

`.pre-commit-config.yaml:86-91` — the hook carries `stages: [commit-msg]`.
`.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml:71-72` — CI runs `pre-commit run --all-files` (default/pre-commit stage only).
`.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml:19-28` — the only commit-message-adjacent CI gate is `Lint PR title`.

## Impact

Airtight under squash-merge (squash subject = PR title = what release-please reads). Under a rebase or merge-commit strategy, individual non-conventional commits reach `main` unvalidated by any required check, and release-please could derive the wrong version bump.

## Fix

If the repo is not squash-only, add a CI step that runs commitlint (or the same pygrep regex) over the PR commit range; otherwise document that squash-merge is required so the PR-title gate is sufficient.

## Resolution

Documented in .pre-commit-config.yaml that the conventional-commit-msg hook is commit-msg-stage only and CI validates convention via the PR title, so the gate is complete under squash-merge. If the repo adopts non-squash merges, add a commitlint CI step over the PR range.
title: Conventional-commit message hook runs only at commit-msg, never in CI

body:
## Problem

`check-version-sync.py` gates commits/CI on version agreement across the five manifests, yet nothing imports or exercises it; only the thin hook wrapper is referenced by tests.

## Evidence

No `tests/test_check_version_sync.py` exists; `tests/test_hooks.py:40` only drives the hook's no-op branches. The mismatch branch `check-version-sync.py:72` `if val != base:` and the module's own copy of `read_toml_version` (`check-version-sync.py:27`, duplicated from the tested `bump-version.py`) are uncovered.

## Impact

Mutations ship green: `if val != base` → `if False`, `return 1` → `return 0` on failure, or a TOML-section regression all pass the suite while the sync gate silently accepts a real desync.

## Fix

Add `test_check_version_sync.py`: a repo with package.json=1.1.0 and marketplace.json=1.0.0 (monkeypatched `REPO_ROOT`) asserts `main()==1` and "MISMATCH" in output; an in-sync repo asserts `main()==0`; a `[tool.x] version` differing from `[project] version` still returns 0 (proves section discrimination).

## Resolution

New tests/test_check_version_sync.py: in-sync=0, mismatch=1, stale second plugin=1, [tool.*] discrimination, single-quoted in-sync=0.
title: The version-desync gate has zero direct tests

body:
## Problem

The hook gate is `path.name == "SKILL.md"` (validate-skill-hook.py:26), so editing skills/nitpicker/commands/<name>.md — breaking its h1, deleting ## When to use, desyncing the table — triggers no validation.

## Evidence

Gate quoted; command files are exactly the surface this branch created, and the validator checks them only when invoked on the parent SKILL.md.

## Impact

The most-edited shipped files in the 2.0 layout have no edit-time guard; violations surface only in CI.

## Fix

Also match `skills/*/commands/*.md` and run validate-skill.py on the parent SKILL.md.

## Resolution

Hook now also matches skills/*/commands/*.md and validates through the parent SKILL.md.
title: validate-skill-hook only fires on SKILL.md — command-file edits are never hook-validated

body:
## Problem

(a) `test_missing_project_version_exits` (lines 81-89) mocks `sys.exit` without `side_effect=SystemExit`, so `update_toml` falls through the exit and writes anyway; the "file unchanged" assertion passes only because the rewrite is byte-identical. (b) `test_unknown_part_treated_as_patch` (lines 52-54) snapshots the unreachable `case _` fallback as contract. (c) tests/test_check_rules_anatomy.py:372-382 relies on `chmod 0o000`, which is a no-op as root (Docker CI) — the test fails there.

## Evidence

Mock without side_effect cannot raise; bump-version.py:71 CLI guard makes "bogus" unreachable; root bypasses file modes.

## Impact

The tests certify nothing about the post-exit write, cement permissive behavior, and are environment-fragile.

## Fix

`mock sys.exit(side_effect=SystemExit)`; drop or invert the unknown-part test; guard the chmod test with `skipif(os.geteuid() == 0)`.

## Resolution

bump-version tests exercise main() against a tmp repo; SystemExit asserted; root-fragile chmod test skipif-guarded
title: assertions that cannot catch the defect they target

body:
## Problem

No linter, formatter, validator, or test parses `.claude/settings.json`,
`.release-please-config.json`, or `renovate.json`. The pre-commit suite lints
markdown (markdownlint), YAML (yamllint), Python (ruff), and scans for secrets
(gitleaks), but nothing checks JSON. `check-version-sync.py` incidentally
parses the four *version-bearing* JSON manifests (package.json, plugin.json,
marketplace.json, release-please-manifest.json), so a syntax error in those
fails CI via `json.JSONDecodeError` — but the three files above are read by no
CI job at all. The only JSON validation that exists, `validate-json-hook.py`,
is a Claude-Code PostToolUse hook: it fires only when Claude edits a JSON file,
never for a human contributor and never in CI.

## Evidence

- `.pre-commit-config.yaml` enables `markdownlint-cli2`, `yamllint`, `ruff`,
  `gitleaks`, and pre-commit-hooks (`detect-private-key`, `end-of-file-fixer`,
  …) but no `check-json` / `check-jsonc` / `pretty-format-json` hook.
- `.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml` runs validators, tests, ruff, and
  `pre-commit run --all-files` — no JSON step.
- `scripts/check-version-sync.py` CHECKS list covers only plugin.json,
  marketplace.json, release-please-manifest.json (+ package.json base);
  `.claude/settings.json`, `.release-please-config.json`, `renovate.json`
  appear in no CHECKS entry and no test.
- `.claude/settings.json:18` is the sole JSON guard and it is a Claude-only
  PostToolUse hook.

## Impact

A contributor adds a trailing comma to `renovate.json` (or `.claude/settings.json`,
or `.release-please-config.json`). markdownlint, yamllint, ruff, gitleaks, the
validators, and the tests all pass; `pre-commit run --all-files` is green; CI is
green. The malformed file ships. `renovate.json` breaking is silent — Renovate
just stops opening dependency PRs with no local or CI signal. This is a
completeness hole in the linting surface, not a hypothetical.

## Fix

The `pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks` repo is already pinned (`rev: v6.0.0`) in
`.pre-commit-config.yaml`. Add one hook under its `hooks:` list:

```yaml
      - id: check-json
```

`check-json` runs on `types: [json]`, which via pre-commit's file identification
excludes the lone `.jsonc` (`.markdownlint.jsonc`, already validated indirectly
because markdownlint-cli2 fails if its config won't parse). One line, no new
dependency, closes all three gaps and is picked up by CI's existing
`pre-commit run --all-files` step.

## Resolution

Added check-json (all JSON syntax) + check-jsonschema hooks (check-github-workflows, check-renovate, plugin/marketplace against local schemas/, check-metaschema) to .pre-commit-config.yaml; runs in CI via existing pre-commit --all-files step.
title: No JSON syntax validation in pre-commit or CI

body:
## Problem

pr.md:21 says "Run through the review checklist (same categories as /nitpicker audit)", but audit.md has no review checklist — its Review scope list differs. pr.md's checklist mirrors review.md's `## Review checklist`.

## Evidence

audit.md scope: correctness, security, reliability, maintainability, performance, tests, docs, conventions. pr.md checklist: Correctness, Edge cases, Error handling, Security, State & concurrency, Data integrity, Resource management, Conventions — matching review.md.

## Impact

Cross-reference points a reader at the wrong command's content.

## Fix

Change the parenthetical to "(same categories as /nitpicker review)" or delete it — pr.md defines its own checklist below.

## Resolution

pr.md now references '/nitpicker review' categories
title: pr.md attributes its review checklist to audit; it mirrors review

body:
## Problem

Six single-line mutations pass the full 254-test suite (each verified by running the mutated suite): (1) findings.py:138 remove `\0` separators from the ID hash input; (2) findings.py:158 drop the `not in_fence` guard so fenced `#` lines become headings; (3) findings.py:273-274 delete the resolve status guard; (4) findings.py:320 delete the `found:` date-format check; (5) validate-skill.py:45 change description length `> 1024` to `>= 1024`; (6) validate-skill.py:117 drop the literal `command` placeholder-row exclusion.

## Evidence

254 passed on every mutated run; originals restored and re-verified.

## Impact

Core contracts (hash-domain separation, fence safety, status validation, boundary conditions) are unpinned.

## Fix

Add one targeted test per mutation, e.g. `finding_id("s","ab","c") != finding_id("s","a","bc")`, a fenced `#` body fixture, `pytest.raises(FindingError)` on `resolve(..., "open")`, a malformed `found:` date, an exactly-1024-char description, and a literal `command` example row.

## Resolution

mutation-killing tests added for the id hash, fence guard, resolve status, date format, 1024 boundary, placeholder row
title: six verified surviving mutations in findings.py and validate-skill.py

body:
## Problem

The ruleset on `main` contains no `required_status_checks` rule, so the `Validate` job in .github/workflows/validate-skills.yml can fail and the PR remains mergeable.

## Evidence

`gh api repos/ivuorinen/skills/rules/branches/main` returns only deletion, non-fast-forward, linear-history, signatures, and pull_request rules — no required status checks.

## Impact

Every CI check (skill validation, findings-store validation, lint, tests, pre-commit) is advisory; a red PR merges.

## Fix

Add a required-status-checks rule for the `Validate` check to the main ruleset. Interaction: a required check that is path-filtered leaves non-matching PRs stuck on "Expected" — drop the `paths:` filter on `pull_request` when making it required.

## Resolution

Added required_status_checks (context: Validate) to the main ruleset 15530545 via gh api; user ran gh.sh and confirmed the rule is present
title: main branch has no required status checks — CI gate is advisory

body:
## Problem

A nonexistent PR number makes the GraphQL path subscript `None`, and the resulting crash is reported as a generic GraphQL failure that then 404s via REST, hiding the real cause.

## Evidence

`fetch-pr-comments.py:153` `page = resp["data"]["repository"]["pullRequest"]["reviewThreads"]`. For an unknown PR, GitHub returns `pullRequest: null` with no top-level `errors`, so the `"errors" in resp` guard (line ~150) doesn't fire and `None["reviewThreads"]` raises `TypeError`, caught at line ~234 as "[warn] GraphQL failed (...), falling back to REST", which then 404s.

## Impact

Correct non-zero exit but a misleading diagnostic — the user sees a REST error instead of "PR #N not found".

## Fix

After the errors check, guard `pr = resp["data"]["repository"]["pullRequest"]`; if `None`, raise a clear "PR #N not found" error.

## Resolution

fetch-pr-comments guards null pullRequest and raises 'PR #N not found'; test_null_pull_request_raises_not_found (replaced the tautological test).
title: Nonexistent PR yields a TypeError and a misleading REST-404 fallback

body:
## Problem

`desc_width = MAX_WIDTH - len(prefix)` can go non-positive; `textwrap.wrap(description, width=desc_width)` raises `ValueError: invalid width 0 (must be > 0)`. `prefix` grows with the longest skill/command name, so a name of ~96+ characters makes `desc_width <= 0` and crashes `make list`.

## Evidence

`scripts/list-skills.py:25-29`:

```python
name_width = max(len(name) for name, _ in skills)
prefix = " " * (2 + name_width + 2)
desc_width = MAX_WIDTH - len(prefix)   # MAX_WIDTH = 100
for name, description in skills:
    lines = textwrap.wrap(description, width=desc_width)
```

## Impact

Latent: current names are far shorter than 96 chars, so this cannot trigger today. It is a dev-only cosmetic tool (`make list`), not shipped to consumers.

## Fix

Clamp the width: `desc_width = max(20, MAX_WIDTH - len(prefix))`.

## Resolution

Clamped desc_width to max(20, ...) in list-skills.py so textwrap.wrap can never receive a non-positive width.
title: list-skills crashes on textwrap.wrap when a name makes desc_width <= 0

body:
## Problem

`.yamllint.yaml` marks `line-length` and `trailing-spaces` as `level: warning` (declared non-blocking), but the pre-commit hook runs `yamllint --strict`, which returns a non-zero exit on warnings as well as errors. The two `level: warning` overrides therefore change only the output label, never pass/fail — a 201-char line or a trailing space is a hard failure despite the declared leniency.

## Evidence

`.yamllint.yaml:6-8,13-14`:

```yaml
line-length:
  max: 200
  level: warning
trailing-spaces:
  level: warning
```

`.pre-commit-config.yaml:36-37`:

```yaml
- id: yamllint
  args: [--strict, -c=.yamllint.yaml]
```

`yamllint --strict` = "return non-zero exit code on warnings as well as errors".

## Impact

Config that reads as intentionally lenient but behaves as strict. A future maintainer relying on the `level: warning` labels will be surprised when a long line hard-fails CI, and cannot tell whether `--strict` or the warning levels is the mistake.

## Fix

Pick one and drop the contradiction: either remove `--strict` from the pre-commit args (honor the `level: warning` declarations), or remove the two `level: warning` overrides (own the strict enforcement). Keeping `max: 200` is fine either way.

## Resolution

Removed the misleading 'level: warning' overrides from .yamllint.yaml (dropped the trailing-spaces block; line-length keeps max 200 at default error level). Behavior is byte-identical under pre-commit's --strict; the labels no longer imply leniency.
title: yamllint 'level: warning' rules are dead config under pre-commit's --strict

body:
## Problem

The frontmatter parser only recognizes list items when indented, so a top-level YAML block sequence — a valid and common style — is discarded, and every path-glob check the tool exists to run is silently skipped.

## Evidence

`check-rules-anatomy.py:61` `if indented and content.startswith("- ") and current_key is not None:`. For valid frontmatter

```yaml
paths:
- "src/**"
- "lib/**"
```

`_parse_frontmatter` returns `{}` (the `- ` items are unindented, so `indented` is False and they fall through to the else at line 79). With `paths` absent, `_check_file` runs none of `absolute_glob`/`traversal_glob`/`stale_glob`.

## Impact

A rules file authored in a standard YAML style passes with zero path validation — a validator false-negative on exactly the surface it is meant to police.

## Fix

Drop the `indented and` requirement: a `- ` item following a `key:` line is a block-sequence element regardless of indentation.

## Resolution

check-rules-anatomy _parse_frontmatter recognizes unindented block-sequence items; path-glob checks now run. Tests: test_unindented_block_sequence_matches_indented + _runs_path_glob_checks.
title: Unindented YAML block sequence under paths: is dropped, silently skipping all path checks

body:
## Problem

`make check` (Makefile:23) runs validate + validate-rules + version-sync + findings-validate + lint + format-check + test, but CI additionally gates on findings INDEX.md currency (`findings.py index` + `git diff --exit-code`) and the full pre-commit suite (markdownlint, yamllint, gitleaks, eof-fixer, mixed-line-ending).

## Evidence

.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml:74-89 vs Makefile:23. CLAUDE.md instructs "run make check before every commit".

## Impact

A locally green commit goes red in CI on a stale INDEX.md or a markdown lint.

## Fix

Add an `index-check` target and `uv run --with pre-commit pre-commit run --all-files` to `check` (update .PHONY and help).

## Resolution

make check now includes index-check and pre-commit targets
title: make check is weaker than the CI gate (index currency and pre-commit suite missing)

body:
## Problem

`build_index` and `validate` disagree on a finding whose `status` is outside {open,fixed,invalid}: validate errors, index silently omits it.

## Evidence

`findings.py:458` `counts[status] = counts.get(status, 0) + 1` counts any status, but the per-auditor row and totals read only `open/fixed/invalid` (`findings.py:467-469`) and the open list requires `status == "open"` (`findings.py:459`). A `status: wontfix` finding is therefore absent from INDEX.md entirely, while `validate_file` reports `invalid status 'wontfix'` (`findings.py:372-373`).

## Impact

A malformed/foreign-tool finding disappears from the index with no signal, so the index can under-report the store.

## Fix

In `build_index`, bucket any status not in the known set into a visible "other" column or emit a stderr warning, so index and validate agree.

## Resolution

build_index warns to stderr on out-of-vocab status instead of silently omitting; test_index_warns_on_out_of_vocab_status.
title: index silently omits findings with an out-of-vocabulary status while validate flags them

body:
## Problem

All six hook commands use a relative `scripts/hooks/...` path and an unquoted `$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` in `REPO_ROOT=`; hook cwd is not guaranteed to be the project root in all invocation contexts, and a path with spaces breaks the assignment. The `REPO_ROOT=` prefix is also dead weight — every hook reads CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR first.

## Evidence

.claude/settings.json:9 and siblings; official guidance is `"$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR"/scripts/...`.

## Impact

Hooks silently fail to run outside the expected cwd or under paths with spaces.

## Fix

`uv run --quiet "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/scripts/hooks/<name>.py"` in each entry; drop `REPO_ROOT=`.

## Resolution

settings.json hooks use quoted absolute $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR paths; REPO_ROOT prefix dropped
title: hook commands use relative script paths and unquoted CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR

body:
## Problem

Line 5 has `argument-hint: "[command] [extra instructions]"`. CLAUDE.md's convention says no behavioral reliance on Claude-only features and names argument-hint explicitly; the router's spec is name + description only.

## Evidence

Dispatch parses free text, so there is no reliance — but the key is one of the two the convention names, and the validator neither requires nor checks it.

## Impact

The repo's own convention reads as violated by its flagship file.

## Fix

Delete line 5, or amend the convention to permit non-load-bearing hint keys.

## Resolution

argument-hint removed from SKILL.md frontmatter
title: router frontmatter carries argument-hint, a Claude-only key the convention names

body:
## Problem

(a) findings.py IDs are 32 bits; on a genuine collision between different (area, title) pairs, `new` suggests `--force`, which would overwrite the unrelated finding. (b) `resolve` writes the resolved copy before unlinking the open one with non-atomic writes, so a concurrent index/validate can transiently see duplicate ids. (c) check-rules-anatomy's `non_md_extension` check (lines 87-88 vs 173) can never fire — `_iter_rules` yields only `.md` files.

## Evidence

~50% collision probability per auditor at ~77k findings; the hook runs findings.py concurrently with edits; dead-code path traced.

## Impact

All latent: silent destruction on hash collision, transient validate errors, silently ignored non-md rule files.

## Fix

(a) Compare existing area/title before suggesting --force; error "id collision with different finding" on mismatch. (b) tmp-file + os.replace, unlink before write. (c) Drop the check or yield non-md files.

## Resolution

collision-with-different-finding guidance and unlink-before-write added; the cosmetic dead non_md_extension check left in place (harmless)
title: shipped-tool robustness advisories: ID-collision guidance, non-atomic resolve, dead extension check

body:
## Problem

`validate-audit-findings-hook.py` regenerates `INDEX.md` by calling
`findings.py index --root <absolute store_root>`. With an absolute `--root`,
`findings.py` writes each finding's path into INDEX as an **absolute** path.
The canonical invocation used everywhere else — `make check`'s `index-check`
and CI both run `findings.py index` with **no** `--root` — writes **relative**
paths. So the hook produces an INDEX that the gate will reject.

## Evidence

- `validate-audit-findings-hook.py:74-78`:
  `subprocess.run([*py, "index", "--root", str(store_root(REPO_ROOT))], ...)`
  where `store_root(REPO_ROOT) = REPO_ROOT/"docs"/"audit"/"findings"` and
  `REPO_ROOT = CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` (absolute).
- Makefile `index-check` and `.github/workflows/validate-skills.yml` both run
  `findings.py index` (no `--root`) → relative paths.
- Reproduced live this session: editing a finding file with the Edit tool fired
  this hook, which rewrote INDEX with
  `.../home/ivuorinen/Code/.../agent-loopholes-040e5327.md` (absolute). The next
  `make check` regenerated it relative and failed `index-check` on the diff:
  `make: *** [Makefile:42: index-check] Error 1`.

## Impact

Any time an agent edits a finding file through the Edit tool (rather than
`findings.py`), this hook silently rewrites INDEX with absolute paths. A commit
of that INDEX then fails `make check`/CI `index-check`, and the absolute paths
leak a machine-specific home directory into a committed artifact. `findings.py
new/resolve` don't trigger it (no Edit tool), which is why it stayed hidden.

## Fix

Make the hook's regeneration match the canonical form. Minimal: drop `--root`
from the hook's `index` call (it already sets `cwd=REPO_ROOT`, so the default
relative root resolves correctly). More robust: have `findings.py index` always
emit repo-relative paths regardless of the `--root` value. Add a test asserting
the hook-regenerated INDEX equals the canonical `findings.py index` output.

## Resolution

Dropped --root from the hook's findings.py index call; cwd=REPO_ROOT resolves DEFAULT_ROOT and emits repo-relative paths matching make check/CI. Added regression test asserting the hook-regenerated INDEX contains no absolute paths and uses the canonical relative form. Closure: hook tests pass; make check green.
title: findings hook regenerates INDEX.md with absolute paths, diverging from canonical relative paths

body:
## Problem

Every main() test in tests/test_validate_audit_findings_hook.py (57, 66, 87) feeds a synthetic top-level `{"file_path": ...}` payload — the shape real Claude Code never sends — so the hooks' production no-op is invisible to make check. tests/test_findings.py has no coverage for: duplicate `new` overwrite, hostile --auditor, newline injection, non-UTF8 store files, fenced blocks in migrate, re-resolving a resolved finding. The `any("id" in e ...)` assertion (105) passes on any message containing "id".

## Evidence

All 241 tests pass while the five reproduced defects exist.

## Impact

Green CI certifies behavior the production wiring never executes.

## Fix

Add one nested-payload test per hook (fails today), regression tests per findings.py defect as fixed, and tighten the id-mismatch assertion.

## Resolution

Hook tests use the real nested tool_input payload (legacy shape kept as fallback test); regression tests added for every findings.py defect; id-mismatch assertion tightened to 'does not match filename'.
title: Hook tests encode the wrong PostToolUse payload shape; findings.py destructive paths untested

body:
## Problem

`resolve_finding` (findings.py:302) unconditionally appends `## Resolution\n<notes>`. On a `--force` re-resolve the body already has one, producing two `## Resolution` headings (markdownlint MD024) and leaving the stale note.

## Evidence

Agent-verified: resolve as fixed "first" then `--force` resolve as invalid "second" yields a file containing both resolution blocks.

## Impact

Duplicate/contradictory resolution notes in the finding file; lint noise.

## Fix

Replace an existing trailing `## Resolution` block instead of appending.

## Resolution

resolve_finding drops a trailing ## Resolution via _drop_trailing_resolution before appending. Test test_force_reresolve_replaces_resolution_without_duplicate.
title: findings.py --force re-resolve appends duplicate ## Resolution section

body:
## Problem

The fence toggle in `migrate_v1` (findings.py:459-465) `continue`s on every line inside a ``` block, so field content spanning a fenced block is discarded.

## Evidence

Reproduced by reviewer: a v1 finding whose Evidence contains a ```python block migrates with the fenced code removed from the Evidence section; surrounding sections survive, masking the loss.

## Impact

The one-time 1.x migration silently destroys evidence — the least recoverable data in the store.

## Fix

Treat a fence as structural only when not accumulating a field value; otherwise append fenced lines to the active field. Extend the V1_DOC test fixture with a fenced block.

## Resolution

migrate_v1 appends fence lines and fenced content to the active field instead of discarding. Test with fenced v1 evidence added.
title: findings.py migrate drops v1 content inside fenced code blocks

body:
## Problem

Lines 5-9 add `uv-lock` and `uv-sync`, but `.gitignore:8` ignores `uv.lock`, so uv-lock regenerates an ignored untracked file (pre-commit's modified-file check can never fail), and uv-sync's default stages (post-checkout/post-merge) never run at commit time or in `--all-files`.

## Evidence

Verified: pre-commit reports `Passed` while silently writing an ignored lockfile; uv-sync absent from `--all-files` output. The project has no runtime deps to lock.

## Impact

Two hooks that look like gates enforce nothing.

## Fix

Delete the uv-pre-commit repo block (or commit uv.lock if lock drift genuinely matters).

## Resolution

uv-pre-commit block (uv-lock/uv-sync) removed from .pre-commit-config.yaml
title: uv-lock and uv-sync hooks are dead gates

body:
## Problem

`parse_rules_frontmatter` (scripts/validate-rules.py:42-60) is a hand-rolled
YAML reader that diverges from real YAML on two legal block-sequence forms, and
in both the `paths:` key ends up wrong or absent -- so the absolute-path
rejection (:118), the `..`-traversal rejection (:121), and the staleness
warning (:128) never run. This validator is the enforcement backbone; its job
is exactly to reject those globs.

## Evidence

Reproduced against the real parser:

- Column-0 block sequence (valid YAML):
  `---\npaths:\n- "/abs/path"\n- "../etc/passwd"\n---` -> parser returns `{}`
  (`"paths" not in fm`), because line 44 requires the item line to be indented
  (`indented = line != content and line[0].isspace()`). A real YAML parser yields
  `paths: ["/abs/path", "../etc/passwd"]`. `validate()` returns early at :104-107
  and never checks the absolute/traversal globs it exists to reject.
- Blank line inside an indented list:
  `---\npaths:\n  - "stale/removed/*"\n\n  - "src/*"\n---` -> parser returns
  `{'paths': ['src/*']}`. The blank line hits `else: current_list = None` (:59),
  so the next item starts a fresh list and reassigns `fm[current_key]`, dropping
  every earlier item -- if the dropped entry was the bad glob, it escapes.

## Impact

Latent today (no current rule file uses either form), but it is a silent
false-negative in the validator that gates `.claude/rules/`: an author can write
valid YAML that the validator waves through while Claude Code's real YAML parser
still applies the absolute/traversal glob at runtime.

## Fix

Fix the parser once (closes both):

- Detect a sequence item by `content.startswith("- ")` while `current_key` is
  set, regardless of indentation (drop the `line[0].isspace()` requirement).
- In the `else` branch, skip blank/whitespace-only lines (`if not line.strip(): continue`)
  instead of resetting `current_list`; only reset on a genuine new top-level `key:`.
Add fixture rules covering column-0 and blank-separated `paths:` lists.

## Resolution

Parser recognizes '- ' items at any indent and no longer resets the list on blank lines. Column-0 lists parse and get path-validated; blank-separated lists keep all items. Regression tests added.
title: validate-rules.py frontmatter parser skips path checks on column-0 or blank-separated YAML lists

body:
## Problem

`migrate` reports success while dropping findings when a v1 file deviates from the exact markdown shape the parser hard-codes.

## Evidence

`findings.py:587` maps only three section headers: `{"open findings":"open","fixed":"fixed","invalid":"invalid"}.get(name,"")`. Any other `## ` heading (e.g. `## Resolved`, `## Open`, `## Won't Fix`) yields `section=""`, and `findings.py:601` (`if m and section:`) then skips every `#### [ID]` finding beneath it. Separately, `findings.py:584` treats any unfenced line starting with `## ` (or `### ` at 591) as a structural boundary and calls `flush()` — so a `## Repro` line inside an Evidence value truncates that finding and resets `section` to "", dropping all findings until the next recognized header. `count` never increments, `migrate_v1` returns 0, main prints "migrated 0 finding(s)" and exits 0.

## Impact

Silent data loss reported as success. Mitigated only by v1 source files being left on disk; a consumer migrating a hand-written or variant v1 store loses findings with no error.

## Fix

When `entry` is set, treat `##`/`###` lines as field continuation rather than structural boundaries. When a `## ` header maps to `""` outside a finding, raise `FindingError(f"unrecognized v1 section {name!r} in {src.name}")` instead of silently continuing.

## Resolution

migrate_v1 now raises on an unrecognized top-level v1 section and treats ##/### inside a finding as field content; test_migrate_rejects_unrecognized_v1_section + test_migrate_preserves_heading_inside_finding_field.
title: migrate silently drops findings under non-canonical v1 markdown

body:
## Problem

The CVE-from-taxa scan assumes every taxon id is a string; a numeric id crashes the run.

## Evidence

`process-sarif.py:171-172` `tid = taxon.get("id", "")` then `if tid.startswith("CVE-"):`. Input `{"results":[{"ruleId":"x","taxa":[{"id":123}]}]}` raises `AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'startswith'`, uncaught — inconsistent with the module's `_int`/isinstance guarding of every other nested field.

## Impact

One non-string taxon id in an aggregated scanner file aborts the whole consolidation.

## Fix

`tid = str(taxon.get("id", ""))` or guard with `isinstance(tid, str)`.

## Resolution

taxon id coerced via str(); test_nonstring_taxon_id_does_not_crash.
title: Non-string taxon id crashes taxa scan

body:
## Problem

`main()` prints `vals[0]` in the success branch without guarding against an empty list. `marketplace.json` with `"plugins": []` yields `vals == []`, so `mismatched` is empty and control reaches `print(f"  OK  {rel_path}: {vals[0]}")`, raising `IndexError`. The `try` at lines 67-72 wraps only `extract(...)`, not the print, so the exception is uncaught.

## Evidence

`scripts/check-version-sync.py:74-79`:

```python
mismatched = [v for v in vals if v != base]
if mismatched:
    print(f"  MISMATCH  {rel_path}: {mismatched} (expected {base})")
    fail = True
else:
    print(f"  OK        {rel_path}: {vals[0]}")   # IndexError when vals == []
```

The marketplace.json extractor is `lambda o: [p["version"] for p in o["plugins"]]` (line 20) → `[]` for an empty `plugins`.

## Impact

`marketplace.schema.json` enforces `minItems: 1`, so the empty-plugins state is caught at commit/CI. But the PostToolUse hook `check-version-sync-hook.py` runs the checker on the raw interactive edit before any schema gate: an edit that empties `plugins` makes the checker die with a traceback; the hook then hits `result.returncode != 0` with no MISMATCH/ERROR lines and `sys.exit(2)`s, blocking the edit with a Python stack trace instead of a clean message.

## Fix

Guard the empty case in the OK branch: add `if not vals: print(ERROR ...); fail = True` before the `elif mismatched:` / `else` branches so an empty version list reports cleanly instead of indexing `vals[0]`.

## Resolution

Added an empty-vals guard before the OK/mismatch branches in check-version-sync.py so an empty plugins list reports a clean ERROR instead of indexing vals[0].
title: check-version-sync crashes with IndexError on empty plugins array

body:
## Problem

`test_query_requests_100_comments_with_page_info` asserts that the query string contains substrings of itself, verifying no behavior.

## Evidence

`tests/test_fetch_pr_comments.py:358-360` `assert "comments(first: 100)" in _mod._GRAPHQL_QUERY` / `assert "pageInfo { hasNextPage }" in _mod._GRAPHQL_QUERY`. It fails only if someone edits the literal, not if truncation logic breaks; the real behavior is already covered by `test_inner_comment_page_truncation_warns` (`:348`).

## Impact

Dead test that gives false coverage credit.

## Fix

Delete it as redundant, or replace with a behavioral assertion about truncation reporting.

## Resolution

Tautological test deleted, replaced with behavioral test_null_pull_request_raises_not_found.
title: Tautological assertion mirrors the source constant

body:
## Problem

`_flush_v1` (findings.py:484-486) writes each converted finding with `path.write_text(...)` and no existence check, so two v1 sources that map to the same auditor and contain the same legacy ID silently overwrite each other.

## Evidence

`security-findings.md` and `security-auditor-findings.md` both map to auditor `security` via `V1_AUDITOR_MAP`. With `#### [N-1]` in both, `findings.py migrate a b` reports "migrated 1 finding(s)" twice and "total: 2", but only one `security/open/N-1.md` exists — the first finding's content is destroyed without warning.

## Impact

Silent data loss during the exact operation that exists to preserve v1 findings; the inflated count masks the loss.

## Fix

In `_flush_v1`, check `path.exists()` and the sibling `open`/`resolved` path before writing; raise `FindingError(f"duplicate id {fid} while migrating {src_name}")` or disambiguate with a suffix.

## Resolution

_flush_v1 now raises FindingError on a pre-existing id in either status dir; test added
title: migrate silently overwrites findings with duplicate legacy IDs

body:
## Problem

Only `ruleId` is read; SARIF 2.1.0 permits a result to reference its rule via `ruleIndex` into `driver.rules[]` instead, and those results are mis-severitied and stripped of metadata.

## Evidence

`process-sarif.py:143` `rule_id = result.get("ruleId", "")`; `ruleIndex` is never resolved. For input `{"tool":{"driver":{"name":"cq","rules":[{"id":"R","name":"BigBug","properties":{"security-severity":"9.8"}}]}},"results":[{"ruleIndex":0,"level":"warning","locations":[]}]}` the tool emits `severity=Medium, rule_name=""` — the 9.8 CVSS rule metadata (`rule_meta.get("security_severity")` at line 148) is never found.

## Impact

A critical vulnerability from a spec-valid scanner is reported as Medium with no rule name or help URI, weakening or hiding the finding in the consolidated report.

## Fix

When `ruleId` is empty, resolve `driver.rules[result["ruleIndex"]]` (with bounds guard) to recover `rule_id` and `rule_meta` before calling `_normalize_severity`.

## Resolution

process-sarif resolves driver.rules[ruleIndex] when ruleId is empty; test_rule_index_resolves_rule_metadata + out-of-bounds guard test.
title: SARIF results using ruleIndex instead of ruleId lose severity and rule metadata

body:
## Problem

`validate_commands` only runs `if commands_dir.is_dir()` (validate-skill.py:101-103), so the 1:1 table↔file rule vanishes when `commands/` is absent.

## Evidence

A SKILL.md whose Commands table lists `foo` with no `commands/` directory validates OK (exit 0). Deleting `skills/nitpicker/commands/` entirely would pass `make validate`.

## Impact

The strongest structural guarantee the validator claims to enforce has a whole-directory bypass.

## Fix

Compute `table_cmds` first; when non-empty and the directory is absent, error `Commands table lists N commands but commands/ does not exist`.

## Resolution

validate-skill errors when the Commands table is non-empty but commands/ is absent
title: table-to-file sync unenforced when commands/ directory is missing

body:
## Problem

`migrate` writes findings one at a time and aborts on the first duplicate id, so a second run (or a v1 doc with a repeated id) leaves a partially populated store with no regenerated index.

## Evidence

`_flush_v1` writes each finding immediately (`findings.py:544`) and raises on a pre-existing file (`findings.py:541`, `raise FindingError(f"duplicate id {fid} while migrating {src_name}")`). `migrate_v1` loops over findings; on a raise mid-loop, main returns 1 (`findings.py:743`) before `write_index` runs (`findings.py:746`). Re-running `migrate x.md` after any partial/complete run also aborts on the first already-written id.

## Impact

A failed or repeated migration leaves some findings on disk, others not, and a stale/absent INDEX.md — an inconsistent store requiring manual cleanup. Migration is not safely retryable.

## Fix

Do a dry-run pass that buffers all `_flush_v1` outputs and detects duplicates before writing anything; treat an already-present identical id as a skip (no-op) rather than an abort, so re-running is idempotent.

## Resolution

migrate_v1 buffers all findings, detects in-run duplicates in a dry-run pass before any write, and skips identical already-migrated files (idempotent); _flush_v1 split into pure _build_v1. Tests: test_migrate_v1_is_idempotent, test_migrate_in_run_duplicate_writes_nothing.
title: migrate is non-idempotent and leaves a half-written store on mid-run abort

body:
## Problem

Only `validate-json-hook` has a positive test that a bad input triggers the blocking `sys.exit(2)`; the other three stdin hooks are tested only for silent no-op behavior.

## Evidence

`tests/test_hooks.py:40` parametrizes `STDIN_HOOKS = ["validate-json-hook","validate-skill-hook","check-version-sync-hook","ruff-hook"]` only across the three no-op tests; the sole positive failure test is `test_validate_json_invalid_file_exits_2_with_stderr` (`:84`). The detection→exit-2 body of `check-version-sync-hook`, `validate-skill-hook`, and `ruff-hook` (e.g. `check-version-sync-hook.py:53-73`) is never driven with a genuinely bad file.

## Impact

Replacing any of those three hook bodies with `pass` (hook silently never blocks) keeps the suite green — the hooks' entire reason to exist is unverified.

## Fix

For each of the three hooks add one positive test: feed a mismatched manifest / invalid SKILL.md / unformatted .py with `REPO_ROOT` monkeypatched, assert `SystemExit(2)` and the expected stderr marker.

## Resolution

test_hooks.py: added positive exit-2 tests for validate-skill-hook (no frontmatter), check-version-sync-hook (desynced manifest), ruff-hook (F821).
title: Three of four stdin PostToolUse hooks never exercise their block (exit-2) path

body:
## Problem

(a) skill-format.md requires exactly one h1 in a command file, but the validator checks only the first header (lines 154-157) — a second `# H1` later passes. (b) Fence detection is `line.startswith("```")` (lines 64-69, 146-152), so indented fences never toggle `in_fence` and their content is parsed as headers.

## Evidence

(a) A command file with a second h1 validates OK. (b) A command file with an indented fence containing `# comment` produced the bogus error "header level jumps from h1 to h4".

## Impact

False passes on the h1 rule; false errors (or masked real jumps) around indented fences.

## Fix

Error when more than one level-1 header appears; use `line.lstrip().startswith(("```", "~~~"))` and track the fence marker.

## Resolution

validate-skill errors on multiple h1s and handles indented/mismatched fences
title: parsing gaps: multiple h1s unenforced; indented fences parsed as headers

body:
## Problem

`resolve_finding` round-trips the file through `render_finding` (findings.py:185, 278-287), which emits only the 8 hardcoded keys; any other frontmatter field is dropped.

## Evidence

Add `cve: CVE-2024-1234` to an open finding's frontmatter, run `findings.py resolve <id> --status fixed --notes done` — the resolved file has no `cve:` line and no warning.

## Impact

Silent metadata loss on every resolve of a hand-annotated finding.

## Fix

Preserve unknown keys: render known keys in order, then append remaining `fm` items (with the same single-line safety check).

## Resolution

render_finding preserves unknown frontmatter keys after the known ones
title: resolve silently deletes unknown frontmatter keys

body:
## Problem

Multiple docs state every command file must have a row in "the ## Commands table" (1:1, enforced), but `x-findings-migrator.md` has its row in `## Internal commands` (skills/nitpicker/SKILL.md:113-120). validate-skill.py collects rows from any table, so validation passes while the docs, read literally, declare the repo in violation — and skill-consistency-enforcer following its own instructions would file a false violation.

## Evidence

scripts/validate-skill.py:106-128 (`_CMD_ROW`) scans the whole body, not just `## Commands`.

## Impact

Doc-vs-validator drift produces false audit findings and confuses contributors.

## Fix

Reword all listed locations to: "Every command file must have a row in one of the command tables of SKILL.md (## Commands or ## Internal commands) and vice versa (1:1, enforced by scripts/validate-skill.py)."

## Resolution

reworded to 'one of the command tables (## Commands or ## Internal commands)' across all 6 docs
title: the '## Commands table 1:1' claim contradicts the Internal commands table

body:
## Problem

On an invalid finding file the hook prints the error to stdout and returns exit 0 (validate-audit-findings-hook.py:56-59). For PostToolUse hooks only exit 2 + stderr is surfaced to the agent; exit-0 stdout is transcript-only. The `findings.py index` subprocess result is also discarded (lines 61-66), so a failed regeneration leaves INDEX.md silently stale.

## Evidence

Code path: `print(...)` + `return 0` on validation failure; `subprocess.run(...)` for index with no returncode check. Even after fixing the payload-schema bug, a broken finding produces zero visible feedback.

## Impact

The hook's entire purpose — block/flag invalid findings and keep INDEX.md fresh — is unenforceable.

## Fix

Print validation errors to sys.stderr and sys.exit(2); check the index subprocess returncode and surface stderr on failure. Pin both with tests.

## Resolution

Validation failure now prints to stderr and exits 2; index regeneration failure also surfaces as exit 2. Tests pin both.
title: validate-audit-findings-hook failure path prints to stdout and exits 0 — validation cannot fail

body:
## Problem

CI runs `findings.py validate`, which checks finding files and duplicate IDs but never compares docs/audit/findings/INDEX.md against the store.

## Evidence

validate_store in findings.py contains no index comparison; hand-editing INDEX.md or merging a finding without regenerating leaves CI green despite the file's "generated — do not edit" contract.

## Impact

The generated index silently diverges from the store, and the file consumers read first is the wrong one.

## Fix

Add a CI step: `python3 skills/nitpicker/scripts/findings.py index && git diff --exit-code docs/audit/findings/INDEX.md` (or teach `validate` to diff the regenerated index).

## Resolution

CI step added: regenerate index and git diff --exit-code docs/audit/findings/INDEX.md.
title: CI cannot detect INDEX.md drift

body:
## Problem

(a) bump-version.py:43 `json.dumps` without `ensure_ascii=False` escapes future non-ASCII; lines 79-81 write files sequentially with no pre-validation — a mid-loop failure leaves a desync. (b) check-version-sync-hook.py:39 matches by `path.name` alone, so any `package.json` anywhere triggers a repo-root sync check. (c) validate-skill-hook.py:27-28: since the repo directory is named `skills`, `"skills" in path.parts` is true for every repo path. (d) stop-reminder.py:31-34 misses git-quoted paths with special characters. (e) All five stdin hooks crash with AttributeError on a non-object JSON payload (`null`, `[]`). (f) validate-audit-findings-hook excludes INDEX.md itself, so a hand-corrupted index is not regenerated until the next finding edit; `path` is never resolved, so `..` segments can dodge the store check. (g) validate-skill.py returns early when description is missing, suppressing all other errors for that file.

## Evidence

Each traced to the quoted lines by direct reading; empty-stdin safety was verified separately.

## Impact

Latent fragility; none currently fires on the happy path.

## Fix

`ensure_ascii=False` + parse-all-before-write; match the five exact repo-relative paths; anchor to `skills/`-prefixed relative paths; `git status -z`; `isinstance(data, dict)` guard; resolve paths and include INDEX.md; collect all errors before returning.

## Resolution

isinstance guards on all stdin hooks; bump ensure_ascii + parse-all-before-write; exact-path hook matching; path.resolve; validate-skill collects all fm errors
title: internal-tooling robustness advisories (bump writes, hook matching, stdin shape, INDEX self-edit)

body:
## Problem

The read-and-parse block catches `json.JSONDecodeError` but not `OSError`. A `.json` path that passes `path.exists()` yet cannot be read as text (a directory named `x.json`, or a permission-denied file) makes `path.read_text()` raise `IsADirectoryError`/`PermissionError`, which escapes the hook as an uncaught traceback.

## Evidence

`scripts/hooks/validate-json-hook.py:39-45`:

```python
try:
    text = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    json.loads(text)
except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
    print(f"  INVALID JSON  {path}: {e}", file=sys.stderr, flush=True)
    sys.exit(2)
```

The sibling hooks hand off to subprocess and so avoid this; this one reads directly. The repo's other PostToolUse hooks all fail open on unexpected I/O.

## Impact

Low: the traceback exits 1 (not 2), so the edit is not blocked — the user just sees Python-traceback noise on a rare path. But it departs from the fail-open convention the other hooks follow.

## Fix

Add `except OSError: return` after the `JSONDecodeError` handler so an unreadable path is ignored rather than crashing the hook.

## Resolution

Added 'except OSError: return' to validate-json-hook.py so an unreadable *.json path fails open like the sibling hooks. Verified: feeding a directory named foo.json exits 0 with no traceback.
title: validate-json-hook catches only JSONDecodeError, not OSError on read

body:
## Problem

Per the Claude Code hook protocol, only exit 2 feeds stderr back to Claude; other nonzero exits show stderr only, and stdout is discarded. Five hooks violate this: validate-skill-hook.py:39-45 runs the validator with `capture_output=False` (errors go to the hook's stdout) and exits with the validator's code (1); validate-json-hook.py:41-42 prints "INVALID JSON" to stdout and exits 1; ruff-hook.py:43-45 prints remaining lint errors to stdout and exits 1; check-version-sync-hook.py:55-60 prints mismatch warnings to stdout and exits 0; stop-reminder.py:42-45 (Stop hook) prints its reminder to stdout and exits 0 — a Stop hook only influences Claude via exit 2 + stderr or a JSON block decision.

## Evidence

Write a SKILL.md with a broken Commands table: validate-skill-hook runs, the validator prints errors to stdout, the hook exits 1 — Claude sees nothing and continues. Verified by reading each hook; only validate-audit-findings-hook.py follows the protocol (stderr + exit 2).

## Impact

The repo's entire local enforcement surface (skill format, JSON validity, lint, version sync, stop reminder) is silently non-functional as feedback to Claude — CLAUDE.md's claims about these hooks are not delivered in practice.

## Fix

In each hook: capture output, print failures to `sys.stderr`, and `sys.exit(2)` on failure (mirror validate-audit-findings-hook.py). For stop-reminder.py, emit `{"decision": "block", "reason": ...}` JSON or stderr + exit 2 when modified skill files exist.

## Resolution

all 5 hooks print to stderr and exit 2 (Stop hook too); test_hooks.py added
title: Hook validation failures never reach Claude (stdout + wrong exit codes in 5 of 6 hooks)

body:
## Problem

The GraphQL query (fetch-pr-comments.py:46) fetches `comments(first: 50)` per thread with no inner pagination.

## Evidence

A PR thread with 60 comments yields a thread with 50 comments, no warning; `is_resolved: false` implies completeness. Thread-level pagination exists, comment-level does not.

## Impact

The `cr` command can miss the newest reviewer instruction — the exact content it exists to implement.

## Fix

Request `comments(first: 100)` plus `pageInfo { hasNextPage }` on the inner connection; paginate or emit `[warn] thread <id> truncated` to stderr when `hasNextPage` is true.

## Resolution

GraphQL inner comments now first:100 with hasNextPage; stderr warn on truncation
title: review threads with >50 comments are silently truncated

body:
## Problem

`table_commands()` (validate-skill.py:130-137) iterates `skill_body.splitlines()` raw, unlike every other structural check in the file which routes through `strip_fences()`. A table-like row `| `name` |` inside a fenced ``` example block in a SKILL.md is treated as a real command entry.

## Evidence

Confirmed by inspection: line 133 loops the unstripped body; `_CMD_ROW` matches any `| `x` |`. Agent [verified]: a SKILL.md with a documentation fence containing `| `example` | ... |` yields `ERROR ... Commands table lists `example` but no commands/example.md exists`, exit 1. Reverse direction (a real command whose only row is inside a fence) reports "not in the Commands table".

## Impact

False positive blocks a legitimate commit: validate-skill-hook.py:55-58 turns validator exit!=0 into sys.exit(2), hard-blocking the Write/Edit that merely documented a table row in an example.

## Fix

Feed fence-stripped lines to the scan: iterate `strip_fences(skill_body.splitlines())` inside `table_commands`, or pass the already-stripped body from both call sites (:122, :145).

## Resolution

table_commands now iterates strip_fences(...) so fenced example rows are ignored.
title: validate-skill table_commands scans inside code fences, causing commit-blocking false positives

body:
## Problem

v1 field continuation (findings.py:554-560) skips blank lines (`elif last_field and line.strip()`), merging multi-paragraph fields, and any prose line matching `^(Category|Area|Problem|Evidence|Impact|Fix|Fixed|Notes): ` is captured as a new field.

## Evidence

A v1 open finding whose Evidence contains the line `Notes: this is actually part of evidence prose` migrates with that line absent (swallowed into a `Notes` field that open findings never render); a two-paragraph Problem becomes one paragraph.

## Impact

Content loss during migration, invisible unless the v1 and v2 files are diffed by hand.

## Fix

Preserve blank lines inside a field; treat `_V1_FIELD` matches as field starts only at top level, or carry unknown captured fields into the open body.

## Resolution
